conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a child - Alex, who is 19 years old and lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to understand more about their current situation.

Is Alex currently in full-time non-advanced education or approved training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is in education - he's doing his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful information. A-levels are generally considered non-advanced education.

When did Alex start their current A-level course?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He started in September, so he's been doing it for a few months now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. And when is Alex's 20th birthday? I need to know the specific date to determine eligibility, as there are age-related rules for Child Benefit when a child is in education.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: His birthday is in about 6 months' time, so he won't turn 20 until later this year.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. A few more questions to complete the assessment:

1. Are you the person responsible for Alex - meaning do you have parental responsibility for him or are you the main person caring for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, yes, I'm his parent and he lives with me. But actually, his other parent also lives with us and they already claim Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. That's an important detail.

Just to confirm - is the other parent (who already claims Child Benefit for Alex) currently receiving those payments, and do you all live together in the same household?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. We all live together in the same household, and the other parent is the one who currently receives the Child Benefit payments for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Result

Based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

**Here's why:**

While Alex meets the basic criteria for Child Benefit (he's 19 years old, under 20, and in full-time non-advanced education doing A-levels), **only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child**. Since Alex's other parent is already claiming and receiving Child Benefit for him, you cannot make a separate claim for Alex.

**However, please note:** If you wanted to be the person claiming Child Benefit for Alex instead, the other parent would need to stop their claim first. You could then submit a new claim to transfer the benefit to yourself. This might be something to discuss between you and the other parent if you wish to change who receives the payments.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and in full-time non-advanced education (A-levels), which would normally make him eligible for Child Benefit as he is under 20 and hasn't completed his course. However, Child Benefit can only be paid to one person for the same child. Since Alex's other parent already claims and receives Child Benefit for Alex, and they all live in the same household, the claimant cannot also claim Child Benefit for Alex. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. If the claimant wanted to claim instead, the other parent would need to stop their claim first, and the claimant could then make a new claim.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is the parent of Alex (19 years old, studying A-levels) and lives with him. While Alex meets the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit (under 20, in full-time non-advanced education), the claimant cannot claim because Alex's other parent already receives Child Benefit for him. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child.

Only one person can receive Child Benefit for the same child at any given time. If the claimant wanted to claim instead, the other parent would need to stop their claim first, and the claimant could then make a new claim.
